coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots from fallen leaves of Lonicera caerulea L.: An innovative plant growth promoter and fruit quality enhancer

coredata.dc:description: With increasing environmental pollution and resource wastage, utilizing waste for high-value applications has become crucial. This study explores the preparation of carbon dots (CDs) from blue honeysuckle leaves and their potential in enhancing plant photosynthesis. CDs derived from these leaves have a particle size of ∼2.6 nm and emit blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, making them suitable for foliar spraying. When applied, CDs enter leaf cells and impact chloroplasts, significantly improving photosystem II (PSII) performance and Rubisco enzyme activity. At an optimal concentration of 1000 mg/L, PSII electron transfer efficiency and Rubisco activity increased by 29.84% and 208.12%, respectively, boosting net photosynthetic rate by 60.4%. This treatment also enhanced blue honeysuckle yield and fruit quality, with higher levels of soluble solids, ascorbic acid, flavonoids, anthocyanins, and total phenolics. These improvements were linked to increased sucrose synthesis (up 25.99%) and leaf assimilative capacity (up 25%). Additionally, CDs enhanced post-harvest soil enzyme activity and microbial abundance, promoting nutrient cycling and soil utilization. This study demonstrates that preparing CDs from waste blue honeysuckle leaves not only mitigates environmental pollution but also offers a sustainable, high value use for plant resources. The findings highlight the potential of nanomaterials in improving agricultural productivity and provide a novel pathway for waste reuse.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characteristics of CDs. Schematic illustration of the preparation of the CDs(a). High resolution TEM image (b), XRD patterns (c), FL emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths (d), FTIR spectrum (e) and full XPS pattern (f) of CDs.

CDs promote the growth of blue honeysuckle. The phenotype (a), Stem diameter (b), dry weight (c), fresh weight (d) and plant height (e) of blue honeysuckle after treatment with CDs at concentrations of 100, 300, 500, 1000, and 3000 mg/L, along with the control group (CK) Those marked with different lowercase letters indicate significant difference from the control (p < 0.05). Values are mean ± standard deviation (LSD test, n = 5). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

CDs promoted photosynthesis in blue honeysuckle. (a) Net photosynthesis rate (Pn), (b) transpiration rate (E), (c) stomatal conductance (Gs), (d) intracellular CO2 concentrations (Ci) and (e) Rubisco enzyme activity in blue honeysuckle leaves. Different letters indicate significant differences among treatments (p < 0.05). Values are mean ± standard deviation (LSD test, n = 5). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Effect of CDs into chloroplasts of blue honeysuckle on chlorophyll fluorescence parameters. (a)the estimated maximum quantum efficiency of PSII(Fv/Fm), (b) minimal fluorescence (Fo), (c) the estimated effective quantum efficiency of PSII(Y(II)), (d) electron transport rate (ETR) and (g) non-photochemical quenching (NPQ)of blue honeysuckle leaves. Different letters indicate significant differences among treatments (p < 0.05). Values are mean ± standard deviation (LSD test, n = 5). (e, f, h and i) TEM images of CDs entering the chloroplast. CP stands for chloroplast, CW for cell wall. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

CDs increased the yield and quality of blue honeysuckle fruit. (a) The blue honeysuckle fruits of CK and CDs treatment. (b) Single fruit weight, (c) total acid content, (d) soluble solid content, (e) ascorbic acid (AsA) content, (f) total anthocyanin content, (g) flavonoid content and (h) total phenols content of blue honeysuckle fruits. The significant differences between the control and CDs are marked with different letters (p < 0.05). Values are mean ± standard deviation (t-test, n = 5). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

CDs treatment improved soil enzyme activity and microorganisms in blue honeysuckle postharvest soil. (a) Leucine aminopeptidase (LAP), β-N-acetylglucosaminidase (β-NAG), β-glucosidase (β-GC) and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) enzyme activity. The significant differences between the control and CDs are marked with different letters (p < 0.05). (b) Bacterial community composition and relative abundance of rhizosphere microorganisms at phylum level in blue honeysuckle postharvest soil. Values are mean ± standard deviation (t-test, n = 5). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With increasing environmental pollution and resource wastage, utilizing waste for high-value applications has become crucial. This study explores the preparation of carbon dots (CDs) from blue honeysuckle leaves and their potential in enhancing plant photosynthesis. CDs derived from these leaves have a particle size of ∼2.6 nm and emit blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, making them suitable for foliar spraying. When applied, CDs enter leaf cells and impact chloroplasts, significantly improving photosystem II (PSII) performance and Rubisco enzyme activity. At an optimal concentration of 1000 mg/L, PSII electron transfer efficiency and Rubisco activity increased by 29.84% and 208.12%, respectively, boosting net photosynthetic rate by 60.4%. This treatment also enhanced blue honeysuckle yield and fruit quality, with higher levels of soluble solids, ascorbic acid, flavonoids, anthocyanins, and total phenolics. These improvements were linked to increased sucrose synthesis (up 25.99%) and leaf assimilative capacity (up 25%). Additionally, CDs enhanced post-harvest soil enzyme activity and microbial abundance, promoting nutrient cycling and soil utilization. This study demonstrates that preparing CDs from waste blue honeysuckle leaves not only mitigates environmental pollution but also offers a sustainable, high value use for plant resources. The findings highlight the potential of nanomaterials in improving agricultural productivity and provide a novel pathway for waste reuse.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In the context of the increasingly severe global climate change and environmental issues, efficient resource utilization and environmental protection have become core topics in contemporary scientific research. Every autumn and winter, a vast number of leaves fall worldwide. As a rich source of organic waste in nature, if not properly managed, fallen leaves are often either burned or buried, leading to the wastage of renewable resources and potentially causing environmental problems such as air pollution and land degradation (Bublienko et al., 2020; Guo et al., 2020; Yelizarov et al., 2021; Park et al., 2022). Therefore, transforming these biological wastes into high-value materials not only holds ecological and environmental significance but also provides a new pathway for achieving a green economy.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are a novel class of nanoscale carbon materials, characterized by their small size (1–10 nm), excellent fluorescence properties, and good biocompatibility (Tuerhong et al., 2017; Kang and Lee, 2019; Li et al., 2021b). In addition, CDs possess abundant surface functional groups, exhibiting excellent photophysical and photochemical properties, and have shown broad application potential in agricultural production (Maholiya et al., 2023). Research has demonstrated that carbon dots can regulate plant growth, enhance photosynthesis, and improve plants' resistance to environmental stresses (Li et al., 2023a),as well as boost their resilience to drought, pests, and diseases (Li et al., 2020).

Lonicera caerulea L. (blue honeysuckle) is recognized as a third-generation functional berry species due to its unique taste, high nutritional and health benefits, and ease of cultivation (Gołba et al., 2020; Qiao et al., 2020). Its fruits are rich in bioactive compounds such as phenolics, anthocyanins, and flavonoids, which help neutralize free radicals and mitigate oxidative stress (Senica et al., 2018). Additionally, blue honeysuckle is high in vitamins, polysaccharides, organic acids, and various mineral ions (Caprioli et al., 2016). The fruit is commonly consumed both fresh and processed into juices, jams, and other health-related products. Additionally, honeysuckle is valued in traditional medicine for its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and immunomodulatory properties (Bieniek et al., 2021; Cheng et al., 2023b). These attributes have increased its commercial potential, leading to growing interest in expanding its cultivation. As the cultivation scale of blue honeysuckle continues to expand, promoting its growth and increasing its yield have become key challenges in agricultural production. Furthermore, the large quantity of fallen leaves, particularly in the autumn and winter, is often disposed of as waste, either incinerated or left untreated, leading to the wastage of renewable resources and potential environmental pollution (Tan et al., 2024). Fallen leaves contain substantial amounts of cellulose, lignin, and other organic components, making them a rich carbon source (Naumenko et al., 2022). In recent years, an increasing number of studies have explored the reuse of biomass resources, such as straw, fruit peels, and fallen leaves (Osman et al., 2019; Cho et al., 2020; Kumar Sarangi et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2023a; Niyogi et al., 2024). However, research on the preparation of carbon dots from biomass waste remains relatively scarce.

This study investigates the preparation of carbon dots (CDs) from the fallen leaves of blue honeysuckle. The approach not only facilitates the resource utilization of waste materials, reducing their negative environmental impact, but also offers a potential for low-cost, large-scale production of CDs. The CDs obtained were used to promote the growth and development of blue honeysuckle, enhancing fruit yield and quality. However, the specific mechanisms underlying this promotion remain unclear. Thus, this study will conduct detailed characterization of the CDs derived from the leaves of blue honeysuckle and apply them in different concentrations to the leaves of the plant. The effects of CDs on plant growth, fruit quality (such as anthocyanins, total phenols, flavonoids, and ascorbic acid), soil enzyme activity, and microbial community composition will be examined. Additionally, the study will explore the effects of CDs on the assimilation and allocation of 13C in the leaves and fruits of blue honeysuckle, aiming to reveal their potential mechanisms of action. This research not only provides a green and sustainable method for the treatment of agricultural waste but also offers new technological approaches for crop growth regulation in modern agriculture. It aligns with current societal demands for green development and the circular economy, offering innovative solutions to agricultural environmental problems and contributing to enhanced crop production efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs via hydrothermal process
ce:section.ce:para: Blue honeysuckle leaves served as the raw material for synthesizing carbon dots (CDs) via a hydrothermal process. 0.5 g of dried blue honeysuckle leaf powder was added to 75 mL of distilled water in a beaker and ultrasonically dissolved for 30 min until the solution was fully mixed. The suspension was then transferred to a 100 mL PTFE (polytetrafluoroethylene) reaction vessel and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. After the reaction, the vessel was cooled to room temperature to obtain the crude CDs product. The crude product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min, and the supernatant was collected. Filtration was performed through a 0.22 μm membrane filter to remove large particles. The filtrate was dialyzed for 24 h using a dialysis bag (1000 Da) to remove small molecular particles. The resulting solution was then freeze-dried to obtain the CDs powder, which was stored at 4 °C.

The morphology and particle size of the CDs were examined and quantified using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEOL JEM2200FS, Japan) operated at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrum was obtained using a Nano Photometer (IMPLEN, Germany). Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were recorded with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-280, TIANJIN GANGDONG SCI. & TECH. CO., LTD, China). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was conducted using a Nicolet iS20 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) to determine the functional groups present. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA) to analyze the surface chemical composition and electronic structure of the CDs. Prior to XPS analysis, the carbon dots were vacuum freeze-dried for 48 h to remove excess moisture without altering their surface properties. The crystalline nature of the CDs was characterized using an X-ray diffraction (XRD) device (Bruker D8 Advance, Germany). Additionally, the location and distribution of the CDs within blue honeysuckle leaf tissues were visualized with a biological transmission electron microscope (Hitachi H-7650, Japan).

The seeds of ‘Wulan’ variety were placed in a Petri dish with damp gauze for germination, maintaining a light cycle of 12 h light/12 h dark. After germination, the seedlings were transplanted into seedling trays containing nutrient soil and watered weekly. One month later, uniformly growing seedlings were selected for treatment. A total of five treatments were applied, with each treatment repeated on 12 seedlings. Deionized water was used to prepare CDs solutions at different concentrations 100, 300, 500, 1000 mg/L, which were sprayed onto the leaves every 3 days, for a total of 3 sprays. An equal amount of deionized water was used as control. After 10 days, samples were taken to measure the physiological parameters of the blue honeysuckle seedlings.

For the cuttings, two-year-old blue honeysuckle ‘Wulan’ variety plants were selected. Uniform cuttings were transplanted into pots and watered every two days, with six treatments in total, each repeated on 9 cuttings, for a total of 54 cuttings. Deionized water was used to prepare CDs solutions at various concentrations 0 (Control), 100, 300, 500, 1000, 3000 mg/L, and the leaves were sprayed every 5 days for a total of 6 sprays. After 26 days, samples were taken to measure the physiological parameters and Rubisco enzyme activity (ESI Text S1) of the cuttings.

For the perennial plants in the germplasm resource garden at Northeast Agricultural University, two treatments were applied, each with 5 plants selected based on uniform growth and absence of pests and diseases. Starting from the fruit enlargement stage, deionized water was used to prepare CDs solutions at concentrations of 0 (Control) and 1000 mg/L, which were sprayed onto the entire plant every 5 days, for a total of 6 sprays. At fruit maturity, the fruit was harvested, and quality parameters were measured.

The root length and seedling height were measured using a ruler. Fresh weight and dry weight were determined with an electronic analytical balance. The height of the cuttings was recorded using a tape measure, measuring from the soil surface to the tip of the tallest branch. Stem diameter was assessed using a vernier caliper. Subsequently, the cuttings were taken out of the pots, cleaned with distilled water, and their fresh and dry weights were measured.

The photosynth etic indices, including net photosynthesis rate (Pn), stomatal conductance (Gs), intercellular CO2 concentrations (Ci), and transpiration rate (E), were measured using a photosynthesis analyzer (LI-6400). A light intensity of 1000 μmol m−2·s−1 was applied, and measurements were taken during clear, windless mornings between 5:00 and 7:00 a.m. For each treatment, three seedlings were selected, and three healthy, uniformly growing leaves from each seedling were measured. Each measurement was repeated three times, and the average value was calculated. The parameters related to chlorophyll fluorescence of the blue honeysuckle cuttings were assessed using a Handy PEA plus (Hansatech Instruments Ltd., Norfolk, UK). Healthy, fully expanded leaves of blue honeysuckle cuttings were selected for chlorophyll fluorescence measurements. For each treatment group, three seedlings were randomly selected, and three leaves per seedling were measured, giving a total of nine measurements per treatment. Each measurement was repeated three times for each leaf to ensure reproducibility and accuracy. Prior to measurement, the leaves were dark-adapted for 20 min to allow equilibrium of the photosystem II (PSII) redox state. The data were then averaged for each treatment and compared to the control group.

For each treatment, three plants were selected, and ten randomly chosen fruits were weighed from each plant. The average weight of these fruits was calculated by dividing the total mass by 10 to obtain the individual fruit weight. The soluble solids and total acidity of the fruit were measured using a PaL-BXIACID F5 fruit sugar-acid meter (ATAGO, Japan). The following indices were determined using a microplate reader: ascorbic acid content was measured using a reduced ascorbic acid (ASA) content test kit (Enzyme linked Biotechnology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China); anthocyanin content was measured using a plant anthocyanin test kit (Enzyme linked Biotechnology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China); total phenol content was measured using a plant total phenol (Tp) test kit (Enzyme linked Biotechnology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China); and flavonoid content was measured using a plant flavonoid (Flavonoid) test kit (Enzyme linked Biotechnology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China).

The experiment involved control group (CK) and 1000 mg/L as treatments. For each treatment, three fruiting branches were selected, with a total of six branches used. Fifteen fruits (experimental fruits) were selected from each branch, and treatments were applied during the juvenile fruit stage, with spraying every 5 days for a total of three treatments.

After treatment, the 13C isotope labeling was performed at 8:00 a.m. using transparent plastic film (with 95% light transmittance) to create a labeling chamber. Small fans, reducing iron powder, and the leaves sprayed with CDs around the experimental fruits were enclosed in the chamber, ensuring airtightness. Additionally, the base of each fruiting branch was girdled and sealed. The 13C isotope labeling process began by injecting 1.0 g of Ba13CO3 (13C abundance 98%) into a centrifuge tube containing 1 mol L−1 HCl solution. Every 30 min, additional HCl was added to ensure complete reaction of Ba13CO3 and maintain the 13CO2 concentration, with the labeling process lasting for 4 h. To avoid cross-contamination, three control plants were selected from a distance far enough to ensure no exposure to 13C.

Three days after the labeling, samples were taken for measurement of relevant parameters. The δ13C value was measured using an isotope ratio mass spectrometer (DELTA V Advantage). Prior to measurement, the fruiting branches were cleaned with water, detergent, and a final rinse with 1% hydrochloric acid, followed by three rinses with deionized water. The samples were then killed at 105 °C for 0.5 h and dried at 70 °C until constant weight was achieved. The dried samples were ground using a mechanical grinder and passed through a 60-mesh sieve, mixed well, and stored in bags for further analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of leaf sucrose content and sucrose phosphate synthase (SPS) activity
ce:section.ce:para: Leaves treated with 1000 mg/L CDs and CK were sampled to determine sucrose content using a high-performance liquid chromatograph (1260 Infinity II Prime). Sucrose phosphate synthase (SPS) activity was measured using an SPS assay kit (Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd., Beijing, China).

Soil samples from the CK and those treated with 1000 mg/L CDs were collected and stored at −80 °C for further analysis. The soil samples were ground on dry ice and passed through a 1 mm mesh sieve. Detailed methodologies for the measurement of β-glucosidase, alkaline phosphatase, β-N-acetylglucosaminidase, and leucine aminopeptidase activities are provided in ESI Text S1.

Soil samples from cuttings treated with 1000 mg/L CDs and the CK were collected and stored at −80 °C for subsequent analysis. Total genomic DNA was extracted from these soil samples using the TGuide S96 Magnetic Soil/Stool DNA Kit (Tiangen Biotech, Beijing, China) according to the manufacturer's protocol. The hypervariable V3-V4 region of the bacterial 16S rRNA gene was amplified using the primer pair 338F (5′-ACTCCTACGGGAGGCAGCA-3′) and 806R (5′-GGACTACHVGGGTWTCTAAT-3′). The resulting PCR products were verified through agarose gel electrophoresis and subsequently purified using the Omega DNA Purification Kit (Omega Inc., Norcross, GA, USA). Purified amplicons were sequenced on the Illumina Novaseq 6000 platform, producing paired end reads of 2 × 250 bp. Sequences with at least 97% similarity were clustered into operational taxonomic units (OTUs) using USEARCH (version 10.0). Taxonomic classification of OTUs or amplicon sequence variants (ASVs) was conducted with the Naive Bayes classifier implemented in QIIME2 (Bolyen et al., 2019), utilizing the SILVA database (Quast et al., 2012)( release 138.1) and applying a confidence threshold of 70%. Species richness and evenness, representing alpha diversity, were calculated using QIIME2. Beta diversity, indicating variations in community composition between samples, was evaluated through principal coordinate analysis (PCoA). Differences in bacterial abundance and diversity were statistically analyzed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). Differentially abundant taxa were identified using linear discriminant analysis (LDA) combined with effect size determination (LEfSe). Sequencing data were further processed and analyzed using the BMKCloud online platform (https://www.biocloud.net).

All experimental data were presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD), with each treatment conducted in triplicate or more. Statistical analysis was carried out using Origin 2019b software, and significance was determined at P < 0.05. Differences among treatments were assessed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) performed with SPSS software.

The CDs solution appears as a light-yellow transparent liquid under ambient light and emits blue fluorescence when exposed to UV light at 365 nm (Fig. 1 a). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images reveal that the CDs have a uniform size distribution, with a diameter of less than 5 nm, and an average diameter of 2.6 nm. They exhibit good dispersion and clear lattice structures, with a lattice spacing of 0.184 nm (Fig. 1b) (Wang et al., 2021). To identify the crystalline form of the CDs, X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed. The XRD pattern of the CDs shows a peak at 28.79°, confirming the presence of a crystalline phase (Fig. 1c). For further understanding of the photophysical properties of the CDs, UV–Vis spectrum determination and photoluminescence (PL) analysis was conducted. The UV–Vis spectrum exhibits a prominent absorption peak at 283 nm, indicating the characteristic optical properties of the synthesized material (Fig. S1a). The maximum excitation wavelength of the CDs was found to be 380 nm, with the peak emission at 450 nm under this excitation wavelength. The emission spectra of the CDs at different excitation wavelengths are shown in the figure (Fig. 1d). The position and intensity of the fluorescence emission peaks varied with the excitation wavelength, indicating that the fluorescence emission is excitation-dependent, which supports the hypothesis of surface-state luminescence mechanisms (Hu et al., 2022). The chemical composition of the CDs was further analyzed using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1e) displayed a characteristic absorption band in the 3100–3500 cm−1 range, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H groups. A peak at 2955 cm−1 was attributed to C-H vibrations, while the peaks at 1600–1700 cm−1 indicated the presence of C=O functional groups on the CDs' surface. Additional peaks observed at 1412, 1113, and 1053 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of C-N, C-O, and C-C bonds, respectively. The XPS analysis provided further insights into the surface chemical composition of the CDs, revealing that they primarily consisted of carbon (C, 63.23% at%), nitrogen (N, 3.34% at%), and oxygen (O, 33.43% at%) (Fig. 1f). The high-resolution C1s spectrum displayed peaks corresponding to C=O (288.3 eV), C-O (286.1 eV), and C-C (284.8 eV) bonds (Fig. S2a). The N1s spectrum showed three distinct peaks, attributed to Graphitic N (401 eV), Pyrrolic N (400.1 eV), and Pyridinic N (399.5 eV) (Fig. S2b). Similarly, the O1s spectrum was deconvoluted into two peaks at 532.6 eV and 531.5 eV, representing C=O and C-O groups, respectively (Fig. S2c). The combined results of FTIR and XPS analyses confirmed the presence of a variety of hydrophilic functional groups, including amino and oxygen-containing groups, which contribute to the excellent stability of the CDs in aqueous solutions (Khairol Anuar et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2022b, 2023b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CDs on physiological indexes of blue honeysuckle
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the impact of CDs derived from the leaves of blue honeysuckle on its growth and development, we treated the cuttings with different concentrations of CDs (100, 300, 500, 1000, 3000 mg/L) and observed their growth. Compared to the control group, the application of CDs at concentrations of 100, 300, 500, and 1000 mg/L promoted the growth and development of blue honeysuckle, leading to an increase in the plant height (Fig. 2 a). The promotion effect became more pronounced with increasing CDs concentration, with the most significant effect observed at 1000 mg/L. The treatment with 1000 mg/L CDs resulted in a significant increase in stem diameter (38.31%) (Fig. 2b), dry weight (72.67%) (Fig. 2c), fresh weight (79.81%) (Fig. 2d) and plant height (55.36%) (Fig. 2e) compared to the control. However, when the cuttings were treated with a 3000 mg/L CDs solution, although there was still some growth promotion, the plant height, stem diameter, dry weight, and fresh weight all showed significant decreases compared to the 1000 mg/L treatment. This phenomenon may be attributed to the fact that at higher concentrations, the carbon dots may exceed the optimal threshold, leading to a stress reaction or toxicity in the plants. Similar observations have been reported in previous studies on nanoparticle applications in agriculture (Li et al., 2014; Gong and Dong, 2021). Additionally, we conducted similar experiments using blue honeysuckle seedlings. After spraying with CDs at concentrations of 100, 300, 500, and 1000 mg/L, the results were consistent with those obtained from the cuttings. CDs treatment also promoted seedling growth, with an increase in plant height, leaf size and number, and root length (Fig. S3a). Furthermore, the number and density of root hairs were enhanced. As with the cuttings, the effect was concentration-dependent, with the 1000 mg/L CDs treatment showing the most significant results. Under the 1000 mg/L treatment, seedling height increased by 3 times (311.1%) (Fig. S3b), root length increased by 2.6 times (260.3%) (Fig. S3c), and dry weight and fresh weight increased by 7.7 times (766.8%) (Fig. S3d) and 3.4 times (342.2%) (Fig. S3e), respectively. These findings are consistent with previous studies, Gong and Dong (2021) observed the beneficial effects of CDs on wheat plants, particularly enhancing growth parameters like plant height and root development. In conclusion, CDs derived from blue honeysuckle leaves can promote the growth and development of blue honeysuckle, with the optimal concentration being 1000 mg/L.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CDs on photosynthesis of blue honeysuckle
ce:section.ce:para: To explore whether CDs promote plant growth by regulating photosynthesis, we measured the photosynthetic parameters of blue honeysuckle after treatment with CDs. The net photosynthetic rate (Pn) of blue honeysuckle cuttings treated with CDs increased in a concentration-dependent manner. A 1000 mg/L CDs treatment significantly increased Pn by 60.4% (Fig. 3 a). Additionally, transpiration rate (E) (Fig. 3b) and stomatal conductance (Gs) (Fig. 3c) showed trends similar to Pn, with all these parameters increasing. However, the intercellular CO2 concentration (Ci) showed a slight decrease (Fig. 3d). In line with our findings, previous studies have also demonstrated that foliar application of nitrogen-doped CDs (PNDs) can promote photosynthesis and growth under drought stress. A 5 mg/L PND treatment significantly increased Pn by 206.8% in maize (Wang et al., 2022a). This result is consistent with our observation, suggesting a similar mechanism of action. The enhancement in photosynthetic rate can be attributed to the unique optical properties of CDs. As a nanomaterial, CDs can effectively capture light energy and facilitate energy transfer, thereby improving the efficiency with which plant leaves utilize light energy (Li et al., 2021c; Cheng et al., 2023a). Rubisco (Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate carboxylase/oxygenase) is a key enzyme in photosynthesis and the core catalyst in the Calvin cycle, responsible for fixing atmospheric CO2 into organic compounds, thus providing a carbon source for life (Parry et al., 2013). To investigate how CDs treatment affects carbon fixation in plants, we also measured Rubisco enzyme activity. Compared to the control, foliar application of CDs (100, 500, 1000 mg/L) significantly enhanced Rubisco enzyme activity by 170.99%, 449.6%, and 208.12%, respectively (Fig. 3e). Rubisco is one of the rate-limiting steps in the Calvin cycle, and its increased efficiency accelerates the CO2 fixation process and enhances the rate of organic molecule synthesis, thereby improving the overall photosynthetic output (Stitt and Schulze, 1994; Parry et al., 2007; Yang et al., 2017). Similar to our results, a study by HU et al. showed that CDs treatment can enhance Rubisco enzyme activity in lettuce, with the optimal concentration being 30 mg/L, which in turn promotes photosynthesis in lettuce (Hu et al., 2022). Therefore, foliar application of CDs can enhance photosynthesis and carbon fixation in blue honeysuckle by improving the photosynthetic rate and Rubisco enzyme activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CDs on PSII performance in blue honeysuckle leaves
ce:section.ce:para: Photosystem II (PSII) is a crucial component of plant photosynthesis, responsible for converting light energy into chemical energy (Baker, 1991; Vinyard et al., 2013). Chlorophyll fluorescence parameters are sensitive indicators of plant photosynthesis and can effectively reflect the light energy capture and utilization of PSII (Bussotti et al., 2020). To further investigate how CDs promote photosynthesis in blue honeysuckle, we measured chlorophyll fluorescence in leaves treated with CDs. Fv/Fm, an important chlorophyll fluorescence parameter, represents the maximum quantum yield of PSII photochemistry (Ibaraki and Murakami, 2006). It reflects the health status of PSII, with normal plants typically having an Fv/Fm value around 0.8. Research by Hu et al. showed that after 7 days of CDs treatment, the Fv/Fm in lettuce leaves remained within the normal range of 0.8, with no significant difference between CDs-treated and control plants (Hu et al., 2022). In our study, similar results were observed: after CDs treatment, the Fv/Fm value of blue honeysuckle cuttings did not show significant changes (Fig. 4 a). This indicates that CDs treatment did not cause significant oxidative stress in blue honeysuckle leaves. After treatment with 300, 1000, and 3000 mg/L CDs, the Fo value slightly decreased (Fig. 4b). This decrease may be due to the fact that when PSII is damaged or electron transport is inhibited, the Fo value usually increases (Agrawal and Jajoo, 2021). CDs can enhance the stability of PSII, reduce light damage, and lead to a decrease in Fo, indicating that the PSII reaction center is in a more open state and electron transport is smoother (Foyer et al., 2017). This can reduce damage to the photosystem and protect the plant's photosynthetic ability. Y(II) reflects the actual light energy utilization efficiency of PSII. Under light conditions, a higher Y(II) value indicates higher light energy utilization efficiency by PSII (Murchie and Lawson, 2013). After CDs treatment, Y(II) values were increased compared to the control, with the most significant increase observed at 1000 mg/L. At a concentration of 3000 mg/L, the increase in Y(II) was less significant than at 1000 mg/L (Fig. 4c). The increase in Y(II) after spraying CDs indicates that the electron transport efficiency of PSII was enhanced, improving the actual light energy utilization efficiency during the photosynthetic process (Li et al., 2021c). After CDs treatment, the ETR value significantly increased compared to the control (Fig. 4d). The most substantial increase in ETR was observed at 1000 mg/L of CDs. This suggests that CDs optimize the PSII electron transport chain, accelerating the transfer of electrons from the reaction center to downstream components, reducing light energy loss, and improving the overall efficiency of photosynthesis (Li et al., 2023b). This result agrees with similar findings by Farhangi-Abriz et al., who reported enhanced ETR in soybean leaves treated with CDs, demonstrating the potential of CDs to boost electron transport efficiency in plants (Farhangi-Abriz et al., 2024). In this study, after treatment with 100, 1000, and 3000 mg/L of CDs, NPQ values significantly decreased compared to the control (Fig. 4g). The reduction in NPQ after CDs treatment suggests that CDs enhance electron transport efficiency, reducing the accumulation of excess light energy and thus lowering NPQ values, ultimately improving photosynthetic efficiency (Li et al., 2021d). In summary, foliar application of CDs can promote blue honeysuckle photosynthesis by improving various chlorophyll fluorescence parameters, which positively impacts plant growth and development.

To further investigate whether the foliar-applied CDs solution enters the subcellular structures of plant cells, particularly the chloroplasts, we used transmission electron microscopy (TEM) to examine the ultra-structure of blue honeysuckle leaves treated with CDs. The results showed significant changes in the ultra-structure of blue honeysuckle leaves after CDs treatment. CDs successfully entered plant cells and further entered chloroplasts, the key site for photosynthesis. TEM images revealed that CDs entered leaf cells through the cell wall and plasma membrane (Fig. 4e and f). Interestingly, CDs not only remained in the cytoplasm but also successfully entered the chloroplasts (Fig. 4h and i). Within the chloroplasts, CDs were mainly concentrated around the thylakoid membranes. This suggests that CDs might regulate the function of thylakoid membranes, thereby enhancing photosynthetic efficiency. Previous studies have shown that the nanoscale size of CDs allows them to pass through the pores of the plant cell wall and enter the cell membrane, where membrane proteins (such as transporters) likely mediate the transport of CDs. After entering the cytoplasm, CDs are typically internalized into organelles via endocytosis. Endosomes can encapsulate and transport CDs to subcellular structures, such as chloroplasts. Especially when CDs are surface modified with specific functional groups (e.g., carboxyl, amino), they can effectively reach the chloroplasts via the endocytosis pathway. After entering the chloroplasts, CDs may be distributed within the thylakoid membranes. The surface functional groups of CDs (such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, or amino groups) could interact with biomolecules within the chloroplast, facilitating their localization in thylakoid membranes or other functional regions (Schwab et al., 2016; Ahmed et al., 2018; Lv et al., 2019). Furthermore, no significant damage to the chloroplast structure was observed, indicating that CDs maintain good biocompatibility even after entering the chloroplasts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs enhanced fruit yield and quality
ce:section.ce:para: To explore whether CDs treatment could influence fruit size and quality, we applied a 1000 mg/L concentration of CDs to young fruit-stage plants. The results showed that, compared to the control, fruits of blue honeysuckle treated with CDs exhibited significant improvements in size (Fig. 5 a). The individual fruit weight increased significantly by 17.2%, reaching 0.88 g (Fig. 5b). Since fruit size and weight are typically closely associated with final yield, we hypothesize that CDs treatment could further increase the overall fruit yield of blue honeysuckle. This finding is in line with the study by Yin et al., which found that CDs enhanced the fruit weight and yield of tomatoes (Yin et al., 2024). In terms of fruit quality, the soluble solids content (SSC) in the fruit increased significantly (9.6%) following CDs treatment, while there was no significant change in total acid content (Fig. 5c and d). These findings suggest that CDs treatment can increase the soluble solids content in the fruits, improving their sweetness. This result is consistent with the findings of Jing et al., who also observed a significant increase in SSC in apples treated with CDs (Jing et al., 2024). The total acid content remained unchanged, indicating that CDs might enhance the fruit's sweetness by increasing the sugar-to-acid ratio. This adjustment leads to a more balanced and desirable flavor, improving the overall taste and quality of the fruit (Feng et al., 2022; Ni et al., 2024). After CDs application, the Vitamin C content in blue honeysuckle fruits increased significantly by 28.07% (Fig. 5e), suggesting that the treatment could improve the nutritional value of the fruits. Compared to the control, fruits treated with 1000 mg/L CDs showed significant increases in the content of anthocyanins (97.73%) (Fig. 5f), flavonoids (14.04%) (Fig. 5g), and total phenolics (10.28%) (Fig. 5h). Vitamin C (ascorbic acid, AsA),flavonoids, anthocyanins, and polyphenols have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. They neutralize free radicals, alleviate inflammation, and help protect cells from oxidative damage. These compounds contribute to cardiovascular health and have been shown to suppress tumor growth (Walingo, 2005; Pandey and Rizvi, 2009; Foyer, 2017; Tena et al., 2020). In conclusion, treatment with 1000 mg/L CDs can not only improve the flavor and sweetness of blue honeysuckle fruits but also increase the nutritional content of the fruits, thereby enhancing their overall health benefits. This treatment could significantly contribute to increasing the nutritional and functional value of blue honeysuckle as a fruit crop.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CDs on carbon metabolism and 13C assimilation and distribution in blue honeysuckle leaves
Carbon metabolism, 13C assimilation capacity, and 13C output and retention ratios in blue honeysuckle leaves
ce:section.ce:para: Our study found that, compared to the control group (CK), foliar application of CDs significantly enhanced the photosynthetic carbon assimilate sucrose content and the activity of the related enzyme SPS (sucrose-phosphate synthase). Specifically, sucrose content and SPS activity increased by 25.99% and 198.04%, respectively (Table 1 ). The synthesis of more photosynthetic assimilates (such as sucrose) in the leaves helps expand the pressure gradient between the source (leaves) and the sink (fruits), thereby promoting the transport of assimilates from leaves to fruits (Sha et al., 2020). To explore the effects of CDs on the distribution of photosynthetic products from leaves to fruits, we used a13C isotope labeling technique to track the assimilation and distribution of carbon. Our results show that the 13C assimilation ability (ΔF) in blue honeysuckle leaves was significantly affected by CDs treatment. Compared to the control group, the 1000 mg/L CDs treatment significantly increased ΔF by 25% (Table 1). This increase in assimilation ability indicates that the leaves' ability to fix 13CO2 was enhanced, boosting the production capacity of the source (leaves), and providing greater driving force for assimilates to be transported from the leaves to other organs. Additionally, the 13C isotope tracking results showed that CDs treatment significantly altered the retention and output ratios of the assimilated photosynthetic products in the leaves. Specifically, the retention ratio was greater than the output ratio, indicating that the assimilated products were first retained to meet the developmental needs of the leaves before being transported to other organs. In contrast, the retention ratio of the control group's leaf products (marked leaves + marked stems) was significantly higher than that of the CDs-treated plants. After treatment with 1000 mg/L CDs, however, the output ratio was significantly higher (70.34%) (Table 1). Furthermore, we analyzed the impact of CDs treatment on the 13C absorption by fruits. Compared to the control, the 1000 mg/L CDs treatment significantly increased the 13C absorption in blue honeysuckle fruits by 123.52% (Table 1). These findings suggest that CDs treatment enhances the transport of photosynthetic products from leaves to fruits, promoting sugar accumulation in the fruit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Distribution of 13C photosynthetic products in blue honeysuckle leaves
ce:section.ce:para: The main distribution of 13C assimilates occurred in the marked leaves and marked stems, followed by the marked fruits, with other organs receiving relatively lower amounts. The proportion of 13C assimilates distributed to each organ was closely associated with the organ's ability to absorb and mobilize 13C from the leaves (Ainsworth and Bush, 2011). Compared to the control group, the CDs treatment resulted in a decrease in the 13C distribution rate in the marked leaves and marked stems, while the distribution rate to other organs increased. Specifically, after 1000 mg/L CDs treatment, the 13C distribution rate in the marked fruits was significantly increased (91.63%) (Table 2 ). These results suggest that the foliar application of CDs promotes the transport of photosynthetic products to the fruits during the fruit enlargement stage. This in turn supports sugar accumulation during the fruit maturation period.

Some studies suggest that when CDs are applied via foliar spraying, they can penetrate the epidermal cells or enter the plant through stomata. Furthermore, CDs can move through the plant's vascular system, primarily transported through the xylem via transpiration pull, carrying water and dissolved CDs from the leaves to the stem, roots, fruits, and other plant parts (Khan et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022a; Zhao et al., 2024). Additionally, plants may expel some of the CDs through their metabolic mechanisms or degrade these foreign particles biologically (Liu et al., 2020). To investigate whether foliar spraying of CDs can improve the postharvest soil quality of blue honeysuckle, we measured the soil enzyme activity and rhizosphere microbial diversity of plants treated with CDs (Wiggins et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of CDs on soil enzyme activity
ce:section.ce:para: Compared to the CK, the activity of LAP, β-NAG, β-GC, and ALP enzymes increased by 30.7%, 69.0%, 12.6%, and 4.9% (Fig. 6 a), respectively, following CDs treatment.

LAP plays a key role in organic nitrogen mineralization in the soil, which is essential in protein degradation. It hydrolyzes amino acids and peptides, releasing nitrogen sources for microbial use and promoting the mineralization and transformation of organic nitrogen (Feng et al., 2021; Bao et al., 2024). The increase in LAP activity after CDs treatment may be due to the improvement in the soil microenvironment or enhanced microbial activity, both of which accelerate organic matter decomposition and nitrogen cycling.

β-NAG enzyme is important for the carbon utilization efficiency in the soil and plays a significant role in nitrogen cycling. It hydrolyzes N-acetylglucosamine compounds into products that can be utilized by microbes, releasing dissolved organic carbon (DOC) and amino sugars. These dissolved carbon compounds affect the soil's carbon fixation, release, and transformation processes. The enhanced activity of β-NAG after CDs treatment suggests that CDs may improve nitrogen mineralization by promoting microbial utilization of chitin and other organic nitrogen sources (Bailey et al., 2011; Liu et al., 2024).

β-GC is involved in the degradation of organic matter in the soil, influencing the carbon cycle. It hydrolyzes glucosidic compounds, such as cellulose and lignin, releasing glucose and dissolved organic carbon. This process promotes the decomposition of organic matter and nutrient cycling, impacting soil carbon dynamics. The increase in β-GC activity after CDs application likely results from improved microbial efficiency in organic matter utilization, thereby enhancing carbon breakdown and transformation (Sanaullah et al., 2016; Adetunji et al., 2017).

ALP is crucial in the phosphorus cycle in soils. It hydrolyzes organic phosphate esters into inorganic phosphate, which plants can readily absorb. The increased activity of ALP after CDs treatment suggests that CDs may enhance phosphorus release and bioavailability in soil, thereby improving plant phosphorus absorption and utilization (Thomas Sims and Pierzynski, 2005; Khadem and Raiesi, 2019; Li et al., 2021a).

In summary, the increased activity of these enzymes indicates that CDs treatment likely improves nutrient cycling in the soil by enhancing the breakdown and transformation of key nutrients, such as nitrogen, carbon, and phosphorus. This positive impact on soil enzyme activity suggests that CDs may enhance the availability of essential nutrients and stimulate microbial activity in the soil.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of CDs on soil microbial diversity
ce:section.ce:para: Research on the impact of CDs on rhizosphere microbial communities has demonstrated that CDs treatment significantly influences the diversity and functionality of these communities. High-throughput sequencing of the 16S rRNA V3-V4 regions was performed on six rhizosphere soil samples (two experimental groups) using the Illumina NovaSeq platform, generating 479,623 raw sequences. After quality control and denoising, 330,051 high-quality sequences were retained. These sequences were classified into 12,808 OTUs, with 8671 OTUs identified in the CDs-treated group (CD) and 7854 OTUs in the control group (CK). The dilution curve for blue honeysuckle samples (Fig. S4) showed that the curve leveled off as sequencing depth increased, indicating that the sequencing data adequately captured the species diversity within the samples. This also indirectly confirmed the species richness of the microbial communities (Wang et al., 2012). This suggests that the sequencing data reliably represent the microbial community composition in the rhizosphere soil of blue honeysuckle and provide a solid foundation for subsequent analysis.

Alpha diversity, which evaluates the richness and diversity of species within an individual sample, was assessed in this study. The sequencing coverage for all samples exceeded 99.9%, confirming that the microbial composition was accurately captured. The Chao1 index was used to estimate species richness, reflecting the total number of species present, while the Shannon index was applied to measure species diversity. Higher values of the Shannon index indicate greater microbial diversity within the samples (Grice et al., 2009). The Chao1 (Fig. S5a) and Shannon (Fig. S5b) indices of the CDs-treated group (CD) were significantly higher than those of the CK group, indicating that CDs treatment increased the microbial richness and diversity in the rhizosphere soil.

The PCoA analysis of the microbial communities in the rhizosphere soil of blue honeysuckle, conducted at the OTU level (Fig. S6), revealed clear separation along the first principal coordinate axis (PC1), which accounted for 43.59% of the total variance. The second principal coordinate axis (PC2) explained 16.05% of the variance. The differences in sample composition were primarily driven by PC1. Samples from the control group (CK) clustered on the right side of the plot, while those from the CDs-treated group (CD) clustered on the left, with no overlap in their confidence intervals. This separation indicates a significant shift in microbial community composition due to CDs treatment. Additionally, the tighter clustering and smaller confidence intervals observed for the CD group suggest lower variability within this group compared to the CK group. These findings confirm that the application of CDs substantially altered the microbial community structure in the rhizosphere soil of blue honeysuckle. Taxonomic analysis identified two kingdoms, 39 phyla, 102 classes, 291 orders, 562 families, 965 genera, and 1095 species. At the phylum level, CDs treatment influenced the bacterial composition in the soil. The dominant phyla in both groups were Acidobacteriota, Proteobacteria, and Chloroflexi (Fig. 6b). Notably, the relative abundance of Acidobacteriota increased following CDs treatment. In the CD group, Acidobacteriota accounted for 23.61% of the microbial community, which was 2.36% higher than the 21.25% observed in the CK group. The increased abundance of Acidobacteriota is associated with enhanced carbon cycling and organic matter stabilization in the soil. Members of this phylum are known for their ability to decompose organic matter, release nutrients, and provide energy for other soil microbes, while also contributing to the regulation of soil pH (Gonçalves et al., 2024).

In addition, the relative abundance of Gemmatimonadota and Cyanobacteria also increased after CDs application. Gemmatimonadota is capable of decomposing organic substances such as cellulose and proteins and plays a role in the carbon cycle, promoting the breakdown of organic matter and the release of carbon dioxide. This contributes to the decomposition and cycling of soil organic matter. This phylum is also involved in nitrogen fixation, supporting plant growth and nitrogen nutrition. Moreover, Gemmatimonadota participates in the formation of soil aggregates and structural stability, affecting soil structure and moisture retention capacity (Mujakić et al., 2022). Cyanobacteria are widely distributed in soils and are capable of nitrogen fixation, converting atmospheric nitrogen into forms useable by plants. They also participate in the decomposition of soil organic matter by secreting enzymes and metabolic products, which help degrade organic matter and release nutrients (Abinandan et al., 2019; Alghanmi and Jawad, 2019).

The application of CDs derived from blue honeysuckle leaves significantly altered the microbial community composition in the rhizosphere soil, increasing both microbial abundance and diversity. At the phylum level, CDs treatment particularly impacted the abundance of bacteria related to the soil carbon cycle and nitrogen fixation. The findings suggest that the use of CDs can positively affect microbial communities, potentially enhancing soil nutrient cycling and supporting plant growth.

This study demonstrates that CDs derived from blue honeysuckle leaves can enter leaf cells through foliar spraying, enhancing the electron transfer efficiency of Photosystem II and the activity of Rubisco, thereby improving photosynthetic efficiency, and promoting plant growth and development. In particular, the 1000 mg/L CDs treatment significantly promoted the growth of both blue honeysuckle seedlings and cuttings, enhancing leaf photosynthesis and assimilation capacity, and facilitating the transport of photosynthates to the fruit. This resulted in increased fruit size, weight, and nutrient contents, including soluble solids, ascorbic acid, flavonoids, anthocyanins, and total phenols. Additionally, CDs treatment improved post-harvest soil enzyme activity and microbial community abundance, thereby promoting the cycling and utilization of carbon, nitrogen, and phosphorus in the soil, as well as the degradation of organic matter and nutrient cycling (Fig. 7 ). These findings provide strong support for the application of CDs in small berry crops with nutritional and health benefits.
